nativeRange.habitat: Muddy margins of streams and seasonally flooded wetlands in tropical lowlands; grows submersed or emersed

imageCandidates.notes: Retailer product photo of potted Echinodorus martii showing characteristic broad ruffled/undulating leaf margins. ~500x659 px. Not a free-licence asset; use only for in-house reference, not redistribution.

Wikimedia Commons category for Echinodorus subalatus (the currently accepted name that subsumes E. martii). Reviewer should pick a specific file with a confirmed CC licence; the species is documented in herbarium and field-photo form rather than aquarium-form here, so visual match to the hobby 'Ruffle Sword' is imperfect.

summary: Large South American sword plant with distinctive broad, ruffled/undulating leaf margins; a heavy-root-feeding background or focal-point plant for medium-to-large tanks, undemanding once a deep nutrient substrate is established.
careNotes: Echinodorus martii is the hobby 'Ruffle Sword' or 'Major Sword' — a large Echinodorus from tropical South and Central America (Brazil, Bolivia, Paraguay, Venezuela, Guyana, Mexico, Cuba) found naturally in muddy stream margins. Taxonomic note: modern treatments (Kew/POWO, Wikipedia) consolidate E. martii as a synonym of E. subalatus, and the trade name 'Echinodorus major' is also applied to this plant; the hobby almost universally still markets it as E. martii / 'Ruffle Sword,' which is the name retained here. Adult submersed plants typically reach 30–50 cm tall with broad lance-to-ovate leaves whose edges are conspicuously ruffled or undulated, giving the species its common name and distinguishing it visually from the smoother-edged Amazon Sword (E. grisebachii). Care matches other large Echinodorus: a deep nutrient-rich substrate (target ~8–10 cm) and regular root-tab supplementation are essentially required — multiple sources flag it as a HEAVY ROOT FEEDER that responds strongly to iron supplementation (yellowing leaves indicate iron deficiency). Lighting demand is moderate; the plant tolerates a wide range but develops fuller, more ornate ruffled foliage under brighter light. CO2 is not required but is reported to improve leaf size and density (Wikipedia notes 'benefits from additional CO2'). Temperature 21–28 °C, pH 6.3–7.5, and gH 4–15 are well tolerated. Place midground or background in mid-to-large aquariums; the broad leaf spread quickly overwhelms smaller tanks. Angelfish and discus may use the broad leaves as spawning sites. Like other Echinodorus, newly purchased emersed-grown plants often shed their initial leaves as the rosette transitions to submerged form — this is normal.
breedingNotes: Propagates primarily by runners (horizontal stems) that produce daughter plantlets; plantlets can be separated and planted once they develop their own rosette and visible roots. Crown division of mature specimens is also possible. Will flower and set seed under emersed/paludarium conditions but rarely produces a fully developed inflorescence in typical submerged setups.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Retail care page for Echinodorus martii 'Ruffle Sword': Brazilian origin, moderate light minimum, temperature 70–82 °F (21–28 °C), heavy root feeder needing nutrient-rich substrate and root tabs, iron fertilizer recommended, midground-to-background placement for mid/large tanks, undemanding with broad ruffled/undulated leaf margins used as spawning sites by discus and angels.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Echinodorus subalatus
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Alismataceae, order Alismatales; E. martii treated as a synonym of E. subalatus), full native range (Cuba, Mexico, Central America, Guyana, Venezuela, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay), habitat (mud at stream margins), submersed/emersed growth, and the note that the plant 'benefits from additional CO2'.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Echinodorus major
additional.notes: Used as cross-check for adult size (30–50 cm), temperature (22–26 °C), pH (6.3–7.2), hardness (4–12 dGH), runner-based propagation, midground/background placement, easy care level, and the broad-ruffled leaf morphology. 'Echinodorus major' is one of the trade names applied to E. martii.

propagation: runners / side shoots with daughter plantlets
